nativeRange.habitat: Rocky reef areas, cave entrances, and crevices along drop-offs, typically at depths of 3–45 m

diet.notes: Tends to prefer live or meaty foods; live saltwater feeder shrimp are commonly recommended when first introducing the fish to the aquarium. Once acclimated, frozen preparations such as mysis shrimp, krill, and silversides have been accepted. Feeding 2–4 times weekly in the evening (or at dusk) is generally advised to accommodate its nocturnal feeding habits.

summary: The Marine Betta (Comet) is a striking nocturnal reef fish from the Indo-Pacific that mimics a moray eel's head as a defense mechanism. Generally considered intermediate care; tends to hide until settled and may eat small fish and shrimp.
careNotes: Calloplesiops altivelis is a primarily nocturnal species that tends to hide in caves and crevices during daylight hours, becoming more active at dusk. A well-aquascaped tank with caves, overhangs, and live rock is generally considered essential for this species to thrive. When first introduced, live saltwater feeder shrimp are commonly recommended to encourage feeding; frozen meaty foods such as mysis, krill, and silversides are typically accepted once the fish has settled in. The species has a well-documented mimicry defense: when threatened, it pokes its head into a hole and exposes its posterior, which—with its large ocellus (false eye) near the dorsal fin—resembles the head of a moray eel. The Marine Betta is generally reported to be peaceful with fish too large to eat, but should be considered a predator with respect to small fish and ornamental shrimp. Care level disagreement was noted across sources (LiveAquaria: easy; Bulk Reef Supply: intermediate; Fish Laboratory: difficult); intermediate is used here as the consensus middle estimate. Adult size disagreement was also noted: reef app listed 16 cm while LiveAquaria and FishBase list up to 20 cm; a range of 15–20 cm is used. Captive-bred specimens (e.g., from Biota) are commercially available and may acclimate more readily than wild-caught individuals. Males have been documented guarding egg clutches; females may not display the broader finnage seen in males.
breedingNotes: Breeding has been reported in captivity. Males guard egg masses attached to rocky surfaces. One source notes the species may be capable of sex change; some individuals in all-male groups have been documented transitioning to female, though the mechanism is not well understood. Captive-bred specimens are commercially available, suggesting successful captive breeding is achievable.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (20 cm max TL), temperature range (24.9–29°C), native range (Indo-Pacific: Red Sea to Tonga and Line Islands), taxonomy (family Plesiopidae), and maximum reported age (9 years).

additional.name: Bulk Reef Supply
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (75–80°F = 23–27°C), salinity (1.020–1.026), pH (7.7–8.5), tank size (50 gal), care level (intermediate). Care level and tank size consistent with LiveAquaria.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Listed adult size as 16 cm (vs 20 cm in FishBase/LiveAquaria) and minimum tank of 300 L. Size discrepancy may reflect aquarium-kept vs wild-caught max. Used range 15–20 cm.

additional.name: Fish Laboratory
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (8 in = 20.3 cm), temperature (72–80°F = 22–27°C), lifespan (10 years), care level (difficult). Lifespan averaged with FishBase max age (9 years) to give 8–10 year range.

fish.breedingNotes: Males guard egg clutches on rocky surfaces; captive-bred specimens are commercially available. Sex change has been reported in captive all-male groups, though the mechanism is not fully understood.
